source_report: RC receiver supply chain intelligence — ELRS/Crossfire/Ghost hardware, MCU dependencies, combat receivers

receiver_manufacturers.name: RadioMaster
receiver_manufacturers.market_share_note: Largest ELRS receiver manufacturer — 12% of all receivers

receiver_manufacturers.name: Happymodel
receiver_manufacturers.note: Pioneered ultra-light ELRS receivers (0.4g). EP1/EP2/PP series.

receiver_manufacturers.name: BETAFPV
receiver_manufacturers.note: SuperD diversity receiver — dual SX1280 true diversity

receiver_manufacturers.name: FrSky
receiver_manufacturers.market_position: Legacy dominant. Declining as ELRS grows. Proprietary ACCESS protocol lock-in.

receiver_manufacturers.name: Team BlackSheep
receiver_manufacturers.note: Crossfire Nano Rx is the standard long-range receiver. Declining vs ELRS.

receiver_manufacturers.name: ImmersionRC / Orqa
receiver_manufacturers.market_position: Only NDAA-compliant receiver manufacturer. Ghost protocol — ultra-low latency. Manufactured in Croatia.
receiver_manufacturers.strategic_significance: ImmersionRC Ghost receivers are the ONLY Western-manufactured ELRS-class RC receivers available.

receiver_manufacturers.name: BAYCKRC
receiver_manufacturers.note: Largest Ukrainian receiver manufacturer. Combat-proven ELRS hardware.

International.note: ELRS targets listed without specific country — most are Chinese-manufactured

mcu_breakdown.summary: 60% of receivers with known MCU use Espressif (Chinese) silicon. Only 4% use STM32 (allied). This is the hidden Chinese dependency in 'open-source' ELRS.
mcu_breakdown.ndaa_concern: Even if an ELRS receiver is assembled in Ukraine or by a US brand, the ESP8285/ESP32 MCU is Chinese-designed silicon from a Shanghai company. True NDAA compliance requires STM32-based receivers.

standard_24ghz.note: Standard 2.4 GHz ISM band — most ELRS receivers

combat_non_standard.note: 37 receivers operate on non-standard frequencies (400-500 MHz) designed for EW-contested environments. All are Ukrainian combat receivers.
combat_non_standard.significance: Non-standard frequencies avoid the heavily jammed 2.4 GHz and 5.8 GHz bands used by consumer drones. Ukrainian combat experience drives frequency diversity.

ukrainian_receiver_ecosystem.significance: Ukraine has the second-largest receiver manufacturer base after China. 15 different Ukrainian companies produce receivers — more diversity than any other allied nation. Combat-hardened designs with EW-resistant protocols and non-standard frequencies.
ukrainian_receiver_ecosystem.mafialrs: Many Ukrainian receivers run MafiaLRS, a combat-optimized fork of ExpressLRS with EW hardening features.

ndaa_receiver_assessment.problem: 80%+ of receivers are Chinese-manufactured with Chinese MCUs (Espressif). Even ELRS (open-source protocol) runs on Chinese silicon.
compliant_options: ImmersionRC Ghost (Croatia) — 7 products, proprietary protocol, Western-manufactured
STM32-based ELRS receivers (rare — only ~14 in Forge DB)
ndaa_receiver_assessment.gap: No US-manufactured ELRS receiver exists. ImmersionRC Ghost is the only Western option but uses proprietary (non-ELRS) protocol.
ndaa_receiver_assessment.realistic_path: Orqa/ImmersionRC expands Ghost receiver line. Or: a US/EU manufacturer builds STM32-based ELRS receivers with allied PCB assembly.
